thinking.text:
The script is extracting too many partial commands because each frame captures the typing in progress (characters being typed one by one). I need a different strategy: instead of looking at each individual frame, I should look at the commands that appear COMPLETE in the transcript (not the one being typed at the bottom). The game transcript shows previously executed commands as complete lines. 

Actually, looking at the output, commands like "n", "e", "w", "s", "u", "d" are single letters and they appear correctly. But compound commands are being captured mid-typing like "put so_" then "put solid" then "put solid" etc.

Let me think about this differently. The video shows a person playing Zork. Each command they type is shown at the bottom prompt. When they press Enter, the command is executed and moves up into the transcript. The issue is that OCR captures the typing animation at different stages.

Maybe I should just look at each frame and extract the LAST command in the transcript (before the current prompt), which would be the most recently executed command. Or better yet, I should detect when a command COMPLETES execution by noticing when the prompt changes from having some text to being empty again, or when new game output appears.

Actually, a simpler approach: since the frames are at 1fps and the game is text-based, maybe I can look at the video as a whole and find all distinct commands that appear. The final frame shows "262 moves" - the game records all commands. If I look at the final frame, it shows the end of the transcript. But the earlier commands scroll off screen.

Wait, I have a better idea. Let me look at multiple frames and build a complete set of commands by tracking the sequence as new commands appear in the transcript. I should filter out partial commands (ending with underscore or containing characters that suggest incomplete typing).

Let me terminate this script and write a better one.
